items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.correct: Competitive rivalry, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers — together determining industry attractiveness and potential profitability.
items.misconception: Porter's Five Forces are five business strategies a firm should choose between.
items.random_wrong: Porter's Five Forces are five physical security measures for a factory.
items.why: Five Forces analyse INDUSTRY structure, not firm strategy choices (that's Porter's generic strategies, a different framework). Students conflate the two Porter models.

items.q: January closing balance is £4,000. What is February's OPENING balance?
items.why: It's £4,000. “£0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which would help SPEED UP cash inflows?
items.correct: Offer cash discounts for fast payment
items.why: It's Offer cash discounts for fast payment. “Pay suppliers earlier” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Month 1: opening £1,000, inflows £5,000, outflows £4,000. Month 2: inflows £6,000, outflows £7,000. What is the Month 2 closing balance, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 1000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
